title: A universal and efficient hybrid digital-analog fermionic quantum simulator
authors: Hao-Tian Wei, Kaden R. A. Hazzard
abstract: We present a universal framework to harness fermionic ultracold atom platforms for quantum simulation, showing how variational algorithms on existing hardware can simulate many-body systems well beyond the hardware's native Hamiltonian. Our analysis provides evidence that one can quantum simulate the ground-state properties of a broad class of gapless target Hamiltonians of local observables in a quantum evolution time that grows polynomially with the inverse relative error, $T\sim O(\mathrm{poly}(1/ε))$ up to logarithmic corrections, offering an exponential speedup over na{ï}ve classical algorithms such as exact diagonalization. We provide numerical evidence and theoretical argument that this holds for energy density, density-density, and spin-spin correlations in three qualitatively distinct models -- the repulsive Hubbard model; a Hubbard model augmented with nearest-neighbor attractive interactions, which introduces the phenomenon of pairing; and the Hofstadter-Hubbard model, which introduces a gauge field and fractional quantum Hall physics. This work demonstrates quantum simulation using current fermionic platforms far beyond the models natively implemented in the hardware.
summary: The paper presents a universal framework utilizing fermionic ultracold atoms to simulate complex many-body Hamiltonians beyond the hardware's native capabilities. By employing a hybrid Variational Quantum Eigensolver (fVQE), it successfully models systems exhibiting pairing and topological order. This demonstrates an exponential quantum advantage over classical simulation techniques for characterizing strongly correlated electronic matter.
main_problem: To develop a universal and efficient hybrid digital-analog framework using fermionic ultracold atom platforms to simulate ground-state properties of many-body systems beyond the hardware's native Hamiltonian.
main_result: The proposed fVQE approach achieves an exponential speedup over classical methods for simulating complex physics, successfully capturing phenomena like pairing and topological order in models not natively implemented on the platform.
method: A hybrid Variational Quantum Eigensolver (fVQE) algorithm is employed, where variational parameters are optimized classically based on measurements of non-native Hamiltonian terms derived from local observables.
model_or_system: The primary system is fermionic ultracold atoms in optical lattices/tweezers arrays. Models studied include the repulsive Hubbard model, an extended version with nearest-neighbor attraction (EFHM), and the Hofstadter-Hubbard model (HHM) incorporating gauge fields.
key_observables: Energy density, density-density correlations ($C_n(r)$), spin-spin correlations ($|C_{Sz}(r)|$), and topological order parameters like the Girvin-MacDonald (GMD) order parameter.
important_parameters: The simulation time $T$ scales polynomially with the inverse relative error ($\epsilon^{-1}$), demonstrating an exponential speedup over classical methods; finite-size effects ($L$) are crucial for determining optimal simulation time $T^*$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the variational principle and assumes that the total error is dominated by a balance between quantum simulation error ($\epsilon_Q$) and finite-size error ($\epsilon_L$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the programmable simulator framework, showing VQE feedback loops; comparisons are made between VQE energy error decay versus classical ED scaling for various models (FHM, EFHM).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the fVQE framework by detailing its application to specific Hamiltonians (FHM, EFHM, HHM), demonstrating how non-native terms are measured and incorporated into the variational optimization loop. It then analyzes the scaling of errors and measures complex correlations characteristic of different physical phases.
why_interesting: This work bridges cutting-edge AMO physics platforms with quantum computation theory by providing a concrete, scalable pathway to simulate strongly correlated electron models (like those found in high-Tc superconductors) using neutral atoms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Programmable fermionic quantum simulator framework. (a) Experimental implementation of the fermionic quantum simulator. Neutral atoms are trapped in an optical superlattice or tweezer array, whose effective dynamics are described by the FHM. The superlattice depth tunes staggered tunnelings, the atom-atom interaction provides an on-site repulsion, and lattice parameters – or, for more flexibility, site-resolved tweezers – control the on-site potentials. (b) The feedback loop of the variational quantum eigensolver (VQE). The quantum device measures terms of a potentially non-native target Hamiltonian after executing parameterized Hubbard evolution and feeds the results to a classical...

FIG. 2. VQE relative energy error ϵQ as a function of total evolution time T for various system sizes L for the (a) even-L FHM, (b) odd-L FHM, (c) even-L EFHM, and (d) Lx = 5 square lattice bosonic HHM. The total evolution time T is defined in App. D. Note the plot axes are logarithmic and square-root scales, so straight lines indicate Ae−B √

FIG. 3. Correlations as a function of separation r for Nl = 3 and Nl = 10 VQE final states (solid markers) and ED re- sults (open gray markers). For the L = 14 half-filled FHM, the (a) density-density and (b) absolute spin-spin correlations show that the Nl = 10 VQE captures the expected exponen- tial and power-law decay, respectively, reflecting the FHM ground state’s finite charge gap and the vanishing spin gap. Similarly, for the L = 14 EFHM, the (c) density-density and (d) absolute spin-spin correlations show that Nl = 10 VQE captures the expected behavior of the EFHM ground state, with gapless charge and gapped spin sectors. FIG. 4. The particle current of an Ns = Lx × Ly = 5 × 7 square lattice for the (a) ground state, (b) Nl = 3 VQE final state, and (c) Nl = 10 VQE final state. The bond color and arrow size indicate the current strength, and the vertex radius is proportional to the local particle density.
